feedback_frameworks.description: Deliver criticism by sandwiching it between two positives
structure: End with encouragement or another positive
feedback_frameworks.example: I really appreciate how creative your proposal was—the visuals were excellent. I think we need to work on the budget section to make it more realistic. Overall, you've made great progress, and with these tweaks, this will be a strong pitch.
feedback_frameworks.when_to_use: Giving feedback to someone sensitive to criticism, performance reviews, coaching
feedback_frameworks.caution: Don't be insincere—the positives must be genuine, or it feels manipulative
feedback_frameworks.alternative: SBI (Situation-Behavior-Impact): State the situation, describe the behavior, explain the impact without fluff

feedback_frameworks.description: Give feedback by describing what happened, what was done, and the impact—no judgments
structure: Situation: When and where did it happen?
Behavior: What specific action did the person take?
Impact: What was the result or consequence?
feedback_frameworks.example: In yesterday's meeting (situation), when you interrupted Sarah twice (behavior), it made her feel like her input wasn't valued, and we missed her key point (impact).
feedback_frameworks.why_it_works: Focuses on observable facts, not personality or intent. Reduces defensiveness.
feedback_frameworks.when_to_use: Correcting specific behaviors, when you need to be direct without sounding harsh

de_escalation_language.why_it_works: 'You' statements sound like blame. 'I' statements express your feelings without attacking.

examples: I feel frustrated when meetings start late because it throws off my schedule.
I feel overwhelmed when I receive last-minute requests because I can't plan my day.
I feel undervalued when my ideas are dismissed because I work hard on them.
I need advance notice for meetings because I have other commitments.
I need quiet time in the mornings because that's when I'm most productive.
I need clearer expectations because I want to meet your standards.
When deadlines change suddenly, I struggle to deliver quality work. Can we have a 48-hour notice policy?
When I'm cc'd on every email, I miss the important ones. Can we limit cc's to need-to-know?
When I don't get feedback, I feel uncertain. Can we schedule regular check-ins?

avoid: You make me feel... (no one can 'make' you feel something—you're responsible for your emotions)

email_templates.scenario: Polite but firm: Coworker missing deadlines
email_templates.template:
Hi [Name],

I wanted to touch base about the [project] deadlines. I've noticed the last [X] deliverables were delayed, which has created challenges for the rest of the team who depend on your work to move forward.

I understand things can get hectic, and I'd like to understand if there's anything blocking you so we can problem-solve together. Going forward, could we agree on:

• [Specific expectation, e.g., 24-hour notice if a deadline will be missed]
• [Specific support, e.g., weekly check-ins to catch issues early]

I value your work and want to make sure we're set up for success. Let me know your thoughts.

Best,
[Your Name]
key_elements: Assume positive intent (ask if there's a blocker)

email_templates.template:
Hi [Name],

I appreciate you thinking of me for [task]. However, I'm currently at capacity with [project A] and [project B], and taking this on would compromise the quality of my existing commitments.

I can take this on after [date], or I'd be happy to [alternative: recommend someone else, offer to help scope it for later, etc.]. Let me know what works best.

Thanks for understanding,
[Your Name]
key_elements: No apology necessary—you're protecting your work quality

email_templates.template:
Hi [Name],

I want to make sure I'm meeting your expectations on [project]. Could you provide feedback on [specific aspect]?

Specifically, I'd love to know:
• [Question 1]
• [Question 2]

This will help me [benefit, e.g., adjust my approach, prioritize correctly, deliver what you need].

Thanks for your time!
[Your Name]
key_elements: Make it easy for them to respond

email_templates.template:
Hi [Name],

I apologize for [specific action]. That was my mistake, and I understand it caused [impact on them].

I've already [what you've done to fix it], and going forward, I'll [specific change to prevent recurrence].

Thank you for your patience.

Best,
[Your Name]
key_elements: Acknowledge what you did wrong (be specific)

email_templates.template:
Hi [Name],

Thanks for the invite to [meeting]. Unfortunately, I won't be able to attend as I have [conflict / other priority].

Could you [send notes after / loop me in via email / reschedule]? I'm happy to [alternative, e.g., provide input async, meet one-on-one later].

Thanks!
[Your Name]
key_elements: Keep it brief—no need to over-explain

difficult_conversations.approach: Lead with value, not need. Use market data.
difficult_conversations.script: I'd like to discuss my compensation. Over the past [time period], I've [specific achievements with measurable impact]. Based on market research for my role and experience level, the typical range is [salary range]. I'm currently at [your salary], and I'd like to discuss an adjustment to [target salary] to reflect my contributions and market rate. Can we talk about this?
tips: Propose a number (don't ask 'What can you offer?')
Time it right (after a win, during review cycle)

difficult_conversations.script: I appreciate being considered for [project]. However, taking this on right now would impact [current priority], which I know is critical. Would it make sense to [push back the timeline / reassign part of it / revisit after X]? I want to deliver quality work, and I don't want to over-commit.

difficult_conversations.approach: Private conversation, focus on behavior not character
difficult_conversations.script: Hey [name], can I give you some feedback? In today's meeting, when you [specific behavior], it came across as [how it seemed]. I don't think that was your intent, but I wanted to let you know how it landed. In the future, could you [alternative behavior]?
tips: Do it privately, not in public

Power phrases: I appreciate your perspective. Here's mine...
Let me make sure I understand...
I'll follow up on [specific action] by [date]

tips: Write angry emails in drafts, then delete them. Never send in the heat of the moment.
If you wouldn't say it to their face, don't write it in an email—tone is hard to convey.
End emails with a clear call to action: 'Please confirm by Friday' not 'Let me know when you can'
Use 'we' for collaboration, 'I' for accountability: 'We can solve this' vs 'I made a mistake'
Before hitting send, ask: Is this kind? Is this true? Is this necessary?
